title: Enhancing the LLM-Based Robot Manipulation Through Human-Robot Collaboration
authors: Haokun Liu; Yaonan Zhu; Kenji Kato; Atsushi Tsukahara; Izumi Kondo; Tadayoshi Aoyama; Yasuhisa Hasegawa
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2406.14097 in June 2024; IEEE RA-L publication in August 2024
open_source_notes: A multimedia demonstration is provided, but no code repository was identified in the supplied manuscript.
primary_problem: Planning; Human-Robot Collaboration; Learning from Demonstration
world_model_detail: N/A — environment information is supplied by object detection and depth sensing rather than a learned predictive dynamics model.
horizon_basis: GPT-4 classifies temporally extended tasks and decomposes them into subtasks, each containing multiple executable motion functions.
replanning_online: Yes — environment perception is refreshed between subtasks and failed skills can be replaced through human teleoperation
learning_type: Prompting; Learning from Demonstration; Dynamic Movement Primitives
observation_modality: Language; RGB-D; object detections and coordinates; teleoperation demonstrations
action_space: Hybrid: discrete motion-function selection plus continuous DMP trajectories
benchmark: Custom real-world tasks including cleaning a table, warming food, roasting food, and opening an oven or cabinet
main_contribution: Combines GPT-4 hierarchical task decomposition, visual grounding, and human-taught DMP skills to execute complex real-world manipulation tasks.
novelty: Uses teleoperation not only for intervention but to create reusable trajectory skills that expand the LLM-accessible motion library.
limitations: Performance is sensitive to object-localization error, LLM token variability, incorrect DMP calls, human intervention requirements, and compounding subtask failures.
future_work: Integrate LiDAR point clouds and tactile sensing to improve real-world perception and manipulation reliability.
evidence_world_model: The system perceives current object locations with YOLOv5 and an RGB-D camera but does not predict future environmental states.
evidence_planning: GPT-4 decomposes long-horizon commands into subtasks and then maps each subtask to executable motion functions.
evidence_horizon: The paper explicitly evaluates long-horizon tasks and notes that their success decreases through accumulated subtask errors.
review_flags: Core long-horizon language-planning and HRC paper; not a world-model method.
source_document: Enhancing the LLM-Based Robot Manipulation Through Human-Robot Collaboration.txt

title: Extended Tree Search for Robot Task and Motion Planning
authors: Tianyu Ren; Georgia Chalvatzaki; Jan Peters
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2103.05456 in March 2021; supplied manuscript revision dated October 2021
open_source_notes: Code repository reported by the paper: https://github.com/ttianyuren/eTAMP
primary_problem: Planning; Integrated Task and Motion Planning
world_model_detail: N/A — planning uses explicit symbolic domain models and geometric simulation rather than learned dynamics.
planning_method: Tree Search; Hierarchical Task and Motion Planning
planning_representation: Hybrid: symbolic task skeletons plus continuous geometric motion bindings
horizon_basis: The planner jointly searches multi-stage symbolic operator sequences and all associated continuous motion parameters for task-level solutions.
replanning_online: No — plans are computed before execution in the evaluated framework
observation_modality: Structured symbolic and geometric environment state
action_space: Hybrid: discrete operators and continuous motion-variable bindings
robot_platform: 7-DoF manipulator; PR2 arm; 10-DoF mobile manipulator in PyBullet
main_contribution: Unifies top-k symbolic skeleton generation, skeleton selection, and continuous motion binding inside one extended MCTS decision tree.
novelty: Explicitly maintains diverse backup task skeletons and uses progressive-widening UCT to optimize exploration across both skeleton and motion spaces.
limitations: The experiments stop at the first feasible plan rather than optimizing final motion cost, top-k generation adds overhead on simple problems, and evaluation is simulation-only.
future_work: Not explicitly stated in the supplied manuscript.
evidence_world_model: The framework relies on PDDL-style task descriptions, streams, and PyBullet feasibility checks rather than a learned world model.
evidence_planning: Top-k planning creates candidate symbolic skeletons, after which MCTS searches skeleton choices and continuous bindings in a common decision tree.
evidence_horizon: The benchmark domains require multi-stage symbolic decisions, including eight successive binding decisions in the Hanoi Tower task.
review_flags: Core classical long-horizon TAMP baseline; outside the learned-world-model family but highly relevant to planning comparisons.
source_document: Extended Tree Search for Robotic Task and Motion Planning.txt

title: Factor Graphs: Exploiting Structure in Robotics
year_basis: First published as a Review in Advance on 12 Jan 2021
venue: Annual Review of Control, Robotics, and Autonomous Systems
open_source_notes: No single companion codebase is associated with this broad review.
primary_problem: Survey; Estimation; Optimization; Planning; Optimal Control
uses_world_model: N/A — not focused on learned world models
world_model_detail: Reviews factorized probabilistic and optimization representations for robotics rather than one learned predictive model.
uses_planning: N/A — multiple planning and control applications are reviewed
horizon_basis: The review covers finite-horizon control, trajectory optimization, and motion planning but does not implement one normalized planner.
benchmark: Multiple robotics applications including SLAM, tracking, optimal control, motion planning, and STEAP
main_contribution: Presents factor graphs as a common language for exposing and exploiting local structure across robotics estimation, planning, and control problems.
novelty: Connects factorization structure to elimination, incremental inference, computational efficiency, algorithm design, and emerging differentiable learning systems.
limitations: The review identifies unresolved computational challenges in mixed discrete-continuous inference and does not provide a single end-to-end long-horizon planning system.
future_work: Advance discrete-continuous inference, factor-graph formulations for optimal control and hybrid task-and-motion planning, and integration with differentiable deep learning.
evidence_world_model: Probabilistic motion models can appear as factors, but the paper is a general optimization review rather than a learned world-model contribution.
evidence_planning: The review shows how desired states, controls, dynamics, and collision costs can be represented as factors for optimal control and motion planning.
evidence_horizon: It covers trajectory and finite-horizon formulations broadly without reporting a paper-specific task horizon.
review_flags: Foundational and peripheral to the core corpus; retain for structured-planning background but exclude from method and world-model trend counts.

title: Foundation models in robotics: Applications, challenges, and the future
authors: Roya Firoozi; Johnathan Tucker; Stephen Tian; Anirudha Majumdar; Jiankai Sun; Weiyu Liu; Yuke Zhu; Shuran Song; Ashish Kapoor; Karol Hausman; Brian Ichter; Danny Driess; Jiajun Wu; Cewu Lu; Mac Schwager
year_basis: Journal publication year: 2024; an earlier public preprint date was not established from the supplied manuscript
venue: The International Journal of Robotics Research
primary_problem: Survey; Foundation Models; Perception; Planning; Control
long_horizon: N/A — survey covers high-level and embodied planning
language_conditioned: N/A — many reviewed systems use language
uses_world_model: N/A — visual generative and dynamics models are reviewed among broader foundation-model classes
world_model_detail: Reviews pretrained language, vision, multimodal, generative, and embodied models across the robot autonomy stack.
uses_planning: N/A — multiple planning paradigms are reviewed
horizon_basis: The survey reviews task specification, code generation, value-function grounding, and embodied planning rather than one implemented horizon.
benchmark: Multiple robotics datasets, simulators, and embodied-AI benchmarks
main_contribution: Surveys how foundation models affect robot perception, decision-making, policy learning, task planning, control, embodied agents, and benchmarks.
novelty: Organizes the literature by components of the autonomy stack while emphasizing practical adoption challenges and safety risks.
limitations: The surveyed field is constrained by robot-data scarcity, physical and embodiment variability, uncertainty, hallucination, safety validation, latency, closed-loop distribution shift, and reproducibility.
future_work: Develop robot-relevant pretraining data and objectives, uncertainty and runtime monitoring, rigorous lifecycle safety evaluation, real-time inference, reproducible benchmarks, and better multimodal grounding.
evidence_world_model: The review includes visual generative models and dynamics prediction but treats them as one component of the wider foundation-model landscape.
evidence_planning: It reviews LLM/VLM task specification, code generation, value functions, policy learning, and embodied agents capable of decomposing goals into actions.
evidence_horizon: No single task horizon is evaluated because this is a broad survey.
review_flags: Supporting survey rather than a core world-model paper; useful for foundation-model and deployment-challenge analyses.
source_document: Foundation models in robotics_Applications_challenges_and the future.txt

title: FurnitureBench: Reproducible Real-World Benchmark for Long-Horizon Complex Manipulation
authors: Minho Heo; Youngwoon Lee; Doohyun Lee; Joseph J. Lim
open_source_notes: Code, data, simulator, furniture models, and setup instructions are released at https://github.com/clvrai/furniture-bench
primary_problem: Benchmark; Imitation Learning; Offline Reinforcement Learning; Task and Motion Planning
world_model_detail: N/A — the paper introduces a benchmark and evaluates BC and IQL baselines rather than a predictive world model.
uses_planning: No — no new planner is proposed
horizon_basis: The benchmark tasks last approximately 600–2300 low-level steps or 60–230 seconds, but the baseline methods are direct learned policies rather than explicit planners.
hierarchical_planning: N/A — the task itself has hierarchical phases
learning_type: Benchmark; Imitation Learning; Offline Reinforcement Learning
pretraining: Baseline-dependent — ResNet, R3M, and VIP visual encoders are evaluated
observation_modality: Front RGB; wrist RGB; robot proprioception
goal_representation: Task-specific furniture assembly objective and phase completion
action_space: Continuous end-effector pose command plus gripper control
benchmark: FurnitureBench; FurnitureSim; eight furniture models with single-skill and full-assembly evaluations
main_contribution: Provides a reproducible real-world and simulated benchmark for long-horizon furniture assembly, including more than 200 hours and 5,000 teleoperated demonstrations.
novelty: Combines realistic 600–2300-step assembly tasks, standardized hardware and initialization, 3D-printable objects, a large dataset, and a paired simulator.
limitations: Furniture is simplified and smaller than real furniture, the setup uses one Franka arm, evaluation is mainly single-task, and FurnitureSim retains significant visual and physical sim-to-real gaps.
future_work: Study multitask and multi-embodiment learning, extend to multi-arm and multi-robot collaboration, improve system identification, close sim-to-real gaps, and improve sample and demonstration efficiency.
evidence_world_model: The paper benchmarks BC and offline RL policies and does not learn a predictive environment model.
evidence_planning: Furniture assembly requires high-level ordering and path planning, but the paper itself contributes the benchmark rather than a new planner.
evidence_horizon: Assembly episodes span roughly 60–230 seconds and 600–2300 low-level steps with repeated grasp, align, insert, screw, and regrasp phases.
review_flags: Core long-horizon benchmark. Exclude from method-only planner and world-model counts, but use heavily in benchmark and horizon analyses.
source_document: FurnitureBench_ Reproducible Real-World Benchmark for Long-Horizon Complex Manipulation.txt
